figures.caption: Fig. 3. Illustration of supervision augmentation and global consistency. Supervision maximization is achieved with the mix augmentation to increase the annotated proportion and data variety. Global consistency requires the segmentation result of mixed image and unmixed image to be consistent.

Fig. 6. Performance of segmentation networks trained using partial cross entropy computed from Points scribble form with different number of pixels Npix, with comparisons to fully supervised models (FullSupUNet): (a) and (c) visualize Dice scores with respect to different Npix on ACDC and MyoPS, respectively. The performance of models trained by the Random walk form, with increasing step length l, compared with models trained by DirRandWalk: (b) and (d) show the Dice scores of segmentation on ACDC and MyoPS, respectively, given Npix = nl.
Table 8. Results and comparisons of irregular structure segmentation using Decathlon-BrainTumor dataset.

Fig. 9. Visualization of irregular segmentation of myocardial pathologies on MyoPS dataset. The two slices were from the median cases by average Dice scores of edema or scar segmentation of all compared methods.
Fig. 4. Two under segmentation examples indicated by yellow arrows: (a) foreground classes (left and right ventricles) in ACDC dataset; (b) background in MyoPS dataset.
Table 7. Results and comparisons of irregular segmentation of myocardial pathologies on MyoPS dataset.

Table 5. Results and comparisons of regular structure segmentation on pathology enhanced images (LGE CMR) using MSCMRseg dataset. Note that the results of HELPNet and ScribFormer are taken from their original papers, and it can be difficult to conduct a fair cross-study comparison due to the differences in training strategies and hardware environments.
Fig. 8. Visualization of cardiac segmentation on LGE CMR using MSCMRseg dataset. The two slices were from the median and the worst cases by the average Dice scores of all compared methods.
Table 6. Results and comparisons of regular structure segmentation on BTCV dataset.
Fig. 1. Roadmap of the proposed ZScribbleSeg framework.
Table 4. Results and comparisons of regular structure segmentation on ACDC dataset.
Fig. 7. Visualization of cardiac segmentation on ACDC dataset. The two slices were from the median and the worst cases by the average Dice scores of all compared methods.
Fig. 5. Illustration of the spatial prior loss (Lspatial) for correction of prediction, via class mixture ratios (π) and the spatial prior (with spatial energy).

Table 3. Results in Dice scores and Hausdorff Distance (HD) of the ablation study using ACDC dataset, where the models were evaluated on the validation set. Note that model #8 is ZScribbleSeg. Bold denotes the best result, and underline indicates the best but one in each category.
Table 1. Summary of hyper-parameters used in the proposed training losses.

Fig. 2. Overview of the training losses for the proposed ZScribbleNet, which consists of modeling of efficient scribbles, computation of priors and shape regularization term. The scribble modeling includes mixup augmentation, regularized with global consistency (Lglobal). The prior comprises class mixture ratios (π) and spatial prior. These components contribute to the spatial prior loss (Lspatial) and the shape regularization loss
Table 9. Results and comparisons of 3D segmentation on Decatholon Prostate dataset with 5-fold cross validation. The results were reported on the test set.
Fig. 10. Visualization of results on Prostate dataset. The selected subjects were the best, median and worst cases by the average Dice scores of all compared methods.
